text: Laparoscopic Meckel's diverticulectomy for small bowel volvulus. A thirty-five year old man presented with central abdominal pain and vomiting. A similar episode six months previously resolved spontaneously. On examination the abdomen was mildly distended and tender in the right iliac fossa. A CT scan showed a small bowel obstruction and was suggestive of a Meckel's diverticulum.

During laparoscopy, the surgeon identifies a Meckel's diverticulum with a persistent vitelline ligament attached to the umbilicus, serving as the rotation point for a small bowel volvulus. The engorged segment of small bowel is reduced using atraumatic graspers.

The surgeon performs a thorough inspection of the small bowel from the duodenal jejunal ligament to the ileocecal valve, ensuring no additional pathology is present. With the volvulus reduced, the constricting effect on the small bowel mucin tree is visible.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the vitiline ligament at the abdominal wall. Subsequently, the persistent vitiline artery supplying the Meckel's diverticulum is ligated and divided with the same instrument.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic stapling device to resect the Meckel's diverticulum, ensuring the lumen of the ileum remains intact. The resected specimen is placed into a laparoscopic retrieval bag and extracted through a ten millimeter port.
